title: Heatwave sceptics reveal our climate delusion
gist: Ellen Halliday uses a record UK/Europe heatwave (only the Met Office's second-ever red extreme heat warning) to argue that public denial coexists with broad support for net zero, while political attacks on climate action now extend from the hard right to the centre right, and the US under Trump is dismantling ocean-monitoring infrastructure that underpins climate knowledge.

themes.theme: Extreme heat as a materialising risk

insights.claim: The UK/Europe heatwave triggered only the second-ever Met Office red extreme heat warning (the first in 2022), signifying danger to life for the whole population, not just the vulnerable.
insights.speaker: Ellen Halliday
insights.so_what: Rising frequency of red-warning heat events is a tangible physical-climate risk to UK productivity, infrastructure and mortality.
insights.quote: This week’s was only the second ever issued by the Met Office—the first being in 2022.

insights.claim: The heatwave caused cancelled trains, school and nursery closures, likely lower economic productivity and deaths, demonstrating concrete near-term economic and human costs of extreme heat.
insights.speaker: Ellen Halliday
insights.so_what: Quantifiable disruption underscores physical-climate risk as a recurring drag on economic activity.
insights.quote: Trains were cancelled. Some schools and nurseries closed. Economic productivity likely dropped.

insights.claim: A Met Office fictional forecast for a June day in 2056 based on 2.5C of warming was worryingly close to what is already being experienced, suggesting warming is tracking severe projections.
insights.speaker: Ellen Halliday
insights.so_what: If present extremes already match mid-century 2.5C scenarios, climate adaptation timelines may need to be pulled forward.
insights.quote: it was worryingly close to what we are already experiencing
insights.locator: The Met Office released a fictional forecast

themes.theme: Political and scientific attacks on climate action

insights.claim: Six in 10 Britons still support reaching net zero by 2050, yet a significant number vote for parties that would tear up emissions commitments — a gap between stated preference and political behaviour.
insights.speaker: Ellen Halliday
insights.so_what: Persistent majority net-zero support amid pro-fossil voting signals continued policy volatility risk for UK energy transition investments.
insights.quote: Even when a majority of us—six in 10—still believe that the UK should try to reach net zero emissions by 2050.

insights.claim: Climate action's former cross-party consensus has collapsed, with attacks now coming not only from the hard right but also the centre right of British politics.
insights.speaker: Ellen Halliday
insights.so_what: Erosion of bipartisan support raises regulatory-reversal risk for renewables and net-zero-dependent sectors.
insights.quote: it is under attack from parties not just on the hard right but also on the centre right in British politics

insights.claim: Trump's US government is attempting to dismantle a network of 900+ ocean sensors that deliver critical climate data, just as a powerful El Niño takes hold.
insights.speaker: Ellen Halliday
insights.so_what: Degradation of US climate-monitoring infrastructure is a knowledge and forecasting risk with global implications for weather/agriculture markets.
insights.quote: Donald Trump’s government is attempting to dismantle a network of more than 900 sensors that deliver critical information about the world’s oceans

insights.claim: Fossil fuel interests are reportedly launching coordinated attacks on climate science at the UN climate talks in Bonn.
insights.speaker: Ellen Halliday
insights.so_what: Organised industry pushback at UN talks signals continued friction in multilateral climate policy — a headwind for global decarbonisation commitments.
insights.quote: Fossil fuel interests are reportedly launching “coordinated attacks” on climate science at UN talks in Bonn.
